title: NOC 73310 – Railway and yard locomotive engineers | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 73310 professionals are the high-precision operators of Canada's massive rail network. As a TEER 3 occupation, this group includes both Railway Locomotive Engineers—who move passengers and freight across the country—and Yard Locomotive Engineers—who manage complex switching and coupling operations within industrial yards. In 2026, as Canada expands its High-Frequency Rail (HFR) corridor and moves toward Zero-Emission Locomotives, these professionals are classified as Critical Infrastructure Talent. For immigration, this role is a prime candidate for the Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP) and the 2026 Transport Category-Based Selection in Express Entry.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level3Major Group73 – General tradesSub-Major Group733 – Transportation occupationsMinor Group7331 – Train crew operating occupationsNOC Code73310

duties: Vehicle Operation: Operate railway locomotives to transport passengers and freight over long distances or local rail networks.
Communication: Use advanced communication systems to coordinate with train crews and traffic controllers, ensuring safe scheduling and movement.
Inspection & Testing: Conduct mandatory inspections of assigned locomotives and test all operating controls, brakes, and safety equipment before departure.
Yard Switching: (Yard Engineers) Operate locomotives to switch, couple, and uncouple cars for loading, unloading, and train assembly.
Order Execution: Perform switching operations according to written switching orders or real-time dispatched instructions.
Routine Maintenance: Inspect locomotives for mechanical issues, replenish fuel, and perform basic routine maintenance.
Safety Compliance: Monitor track conditions, signals, and speed limits to strictly adhere to the Canadian Rail Operating Rules (CROR).
Emergency Response: Diagnose mechanical failures during transit and coordinate with maintenance teams for roadside repairs.
Data Logging: Record trip data, fuel consumption, and any mechanical incidents in digital logbooks for regulatory compliance.
Passenger Welfare: Ensure the smooth acceleration and braking of passenger trains to maintain safety and comfort for travelers.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Experience (Mandatory): Experience as a Railway Conductor (NOC 74101) is typically required for promotion to Locomotive Engineer.
Certification: A valid Canadian Rail Operating Rules (CROR) certificate is mandatory to practice in Canada.
Training: Completion of a specialized training program (often 24 months) provided by major carriers like CPKC, CN, or VIA Rail is required.
Physical Fitness: Candidates must pass rigorous medical, vision, and hearing examinations, along with drug and alcohol screening.
Seniority: Most positions are governed by collective agreements, where seniority influences route assignments and scheduling.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, senior Locomotive Engineers on long-haul freight routes or specialized passenger lines often earn annual salaries between $115,000 and $155,000 inclusive of premiums.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate that the expansion of regional rail networks (like the Ontario Line and REM in Montreal) and the growth of cross-border trade under USMCA will sustain high demand. While automation is being tested in yards, the complex decision-making required for main-track passenger and freight operations ensures engineers will remain indispensable through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The demand for locomotive engineers in 2026 is Strong, particularly in British Columbia, Ontario, and Alberta. According to 2026 labor market forecasts, the industry is facing a significant "Skill Gap" as over 35% of current engineers approach retirement. In B.C., regions like Mainland/Southwest and Thompson-Okanagan are reporting record job openings. Nationally, the push for sustainable supply chains has increased rail traffic, ensuring that locomotive engineers remain one of the most stable and high-paying trades in the transportation sector.
Exceptional Compensation: This is one of the highest-paying trades in Canada, with senior engineers frequently earning over $140,000 annually.
Modern Rail Tech: Work with the latest Tier 4 diesel-electric and emerging hydrogen-cell locomotives equipped with Positive Train Control (PTC) systems.
Superior Benefits: Nearly 100% of unionized engineers (Teamsters Rail) receive top-tier medical, dental, and defined-benefit pension plans.
Strategic PR Pathways: This NOC is a priority for 2026 Transport Category draws, allowing skilled operators to secure Permanent Residency with lower CRS scores.
pathways: Express Entry (Transport Category Selection): A high-priority NOC for 2026 category-based draws with lower CRS requirements.
Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP): A reliable route for engineers with 2 years of experience and a valid job offer or Canadian certification.
BC PNP Skilled Worker: Priority for experienced engineers with a job offer from a railway transport company in British Columbia.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: Ideal for those currently employed as conductors or engineers within Alberta’s rail network.
